name: ECS Rails
tagline: An Entity-Component-System reimagining of ActiveRecord that stays idiomatic to Ruby on Rails
target: Rails developers who want composable, component-based domain models without inheritance or polymorphic-association sprawl

log.summary: Added ECS Rails to the portfolio as a placeholder under Developer Tools. An open-source Ruby gem that reimagines ActiveRecord around an Entity-Component-System architecture while remaining idiomatic to Rails. Each entity is a lightweight identity record composed from reusable, lazily persisted components that encapsulate both data and behaviour; components are queried and processed independently through Systems, enabling composable domain models, eliminating much of the need for inheritance and polymorphic associations, and letting background processing operate directly on relevant data. Ships as a production-ready gem plus a companion bulletin-board application demonstrating how complex Rails apps can be built from reusable components while retaining Rails conventions, DX, and ecosystem.

Turned proposal.html into a specification and shipped the first two RFCs. Interviewed to settle the design, then wrote docs/architecture.md (the binding invariants), seven ADRs, eight RFCs sizing the v0.1 build, and a design backlog. Scaffolded the ecs_rails gem (MIT, Ruby 3.4.5 via rbenv, RSpec against real PostgreSQL) and a lean Rails 8.1 bulletin-board demo consuming it via a path: dependency, so the PROCESS.md gem-to-demo feedback loop has no bundle step in it. Delegated RFC-0001 (ApplicationEntity, entity identity, model discriminator) and RFC-0002 (reload-safe component registry) to parallel sub-agents; 55 examples green. The agents found four defects in the spec itself, all now corrected: attr_readonly does not raise in Rails 8 without a hand-rolled guard; a per-subclass default_scope would accumulate and silently break sub-subclasses; RFC-0002 said registration was both idempotent and raising; and the registry's name-keying makes anonymous entity classes unregisterable, which invalidated RFC-0002's and RFC-0004's own example tests. Two new open questions logged, one of which blocks RFC-0003: entities.model does not resolve back to a subclass on read, so ApplicationEntity.find returns an ApplicationEntity rather than a User — undercutting ADR-0002's core justification.

Phase 2: RFC-0003 (ApplicationComponent, entity subclass resolution) and RFC-0008 (install and component generators) built by parallel sub-agents. 157 examples green. Half of v0.1 is now landed (RFCs 0001, 0002, 0003, 0008). The headline finding is that ADR-0008 was wrong on both counts and had to be amended: overriding discriminate_class_for_record is dead code because ActiveRecord gates the hook on the result set containing inheritance_column, which the entities table has no reason to have — so Option A required the very gate that only the rejected Option B opens; fixed via the private instantiate_instance_of seam, now logged as an open question about depending on private Rails API. Separately, the ADR worried about the wrong failure mode: every irregular inflection round-trips fine (people, data, equipment, series, analyses), but namespacing is total and unfixable by inflection, because model_name.plural underscores the whole constant path before the inflector is consulted and is not injective (Blog::Post and BlogPost both give 'blog_posts'). Switched the discriminator to model_name.collection, which inverts cleanly and is byte-identical for every non-namespaced class, so no data migration was needed. The generator agent also found two require bugs inherited from Rails' own generator files, masked by an unrelated require in registry.rb. Both ADR errors were internally coherent, would have survived review, and were only caught by execution.

Phase 4: RFC-0006 (lazy components) and RFC-0005 (method delegation) landed, sequentially — I'd twice mislabelled these as independent siblings, but delegation writers (user.address = 'x') need user.email to be an instance, and 0004's reader returns nil, so 0005 genuinely depends on 0006. Corrected the dependency graph. 339 examples green. The whole proposal now works end to end: user.email is never nil, reading it costs one SELECT and inserts nothing, user.email.address = 'x'; user.save! writes exactly one row, user.send_welcome_email delegates with self bound to the component, and name clashes between components raise at load time with the fix quoted. Two more ADR-grade findings, both from running the code rather than reading it. First, ADR-0003's claim that dirty-tracking 'follows ActiveModel rather than inventing new semantics' is provably false: a virtual component has entity_id set, which ActiveModel counts as a change, so deferring to changed? would insert a row for every component ever read — the gem must define its own dirty rule that excludes identity columns. That is the second approved ADR (after 0008) falsified by its own dependency; both assumed Rails already did the work. Second, the test harness itself was hiding rollbacks: spec_helper wrapped each example in a joinable transaction, so inner transactions merged instead of taking savepoints and rollbacks were swallowed — every atomicity assertion in the suite had been passing whether the code rolled back or not. Fixed with joinable: false, confirmed load-bearing. Also: except:/only: had to cover the whole accessor pair (title implies title=) or models.rb's own declaration could not load, and the delegated-set formula the RFC suggested would have delegated ~140 AR-generated dirty-tracking methods. Only RFC-0007 (validation error merging) remains for v0.1; the save/save! contract is already correct and atomic, so 0007's job is narrowed to making valid? reflect component validity and merging errors for forms.

Phase 5: RFC-0007 (validation error merging) landed — v0.1 is now API-complete, all eight RFCs implemented, 382 examples green. entity.valid? reflects its touched components' validity and errors merge so a form reads normally (user.errors[:'email.address'] => ['is invalid'], full_messages => ['Email address is invalid']). Scope was narrower than the RFC first stated, because RFC-0006 had already made save/save! correct and atomic; the job reduced to making valid? fail first rather than the cascade's component.save! raising, and decoupling the machine-readable error key from the human message (which ActiveModel couples). valid? validates only components already in the entity's memo, and provably has no side effects. The agent surfaced the registry-singleton landmine a third time — its first cut read the registry from human_attribute_name, a hot per-form-field method, which is fragile in production because the Railtie clears the registry on reload; fixed by keying on the durable generated reader method instead. I then sealed the root cause centrally: added Registry#snapshot/#restore and a global spec_helper hook that restores the models.rb baseline after every example, so no spec can leak a cleared registry regardless of order. Also untracked .claude/settings.local.json, which the harness kept rewriting. Next: build the bulletin-board demo for real against the finished gem — the actual v0.1 kill/keep question of whether modelling with components is pleasant.

Demo phase (models + sharp cases), driven iteratively with a friction log as the deliverable — the v0.1 kill/keep question of whether modelling with components is pleasant. Modelled the whole bulletin board (Users, Posts, Comments, Groups, Memberships) against the finished gem. The verdict: the core claims hold cleanly — shared component types with shared behaviour (Likes on Post+Comment, Name on User+Group), relationship components, and the join-entity pattern for many-to-many all just work. The demo drove two real gem improvements. First, a 🔴 infinite-recursion bug: the gem's OWN ADR-0006 example (class Author; belongs_to :author) stack-overflowed, because the component reader `author` and the delegated belongs_to `author` collided and the delegation silently overwrote the reader with a method that called itself; fixed to raise a reader collision at declaration (ADR-0004 stance), and the fix pointed at a nicer model (name the component Authorship, the association author, so post.author returns the User). Second, a 🔴 blocker for the proposal's 'No STI' headline: marker components (Moderator/Administrator) have no state, so the lazy cascade never persists them and user.moderator; user.save! silently did nothing. Added a first-class presence API (ADR-0009/RFC-0009): entity.add/has?/remove plus a generated entity.<component>? predicate. Markers now work — a user is a moderator exactly when the row exists. Also decided to keep attribute auto-delegation automatic (collisions raise loudly, so except:-on-both is the documented pattern) rather than make it opt-in. Gem at 424 examples green. Two ADRs amended by their own demo: ADR-0006 (relationship naming) and ADR-0009 (predicate collisions defer, not raise). Next: optionally build controllers/views, or consolidate.

Built the component query DSL (with_component / without_component) — the proposal's hardest feature and the demo's #1 confirmed need. RFC-0010 + ADR-0011, implemented via a sub-agent, 457 gem examples green. Two demo-surfaced constraints drove the design and both are handled. First, verb naming: the proposal's .with and .without are BOTH already ActiveRecord methods (CTEs and the excluding alias), and even .composed_of is taken (aggregations) — so the whole proposed query syntax collides with Rails; the free, unambiguous with_component/without_component verbs are used instead. Second, cross-entity leakage: component tables are shared across entity types (PublishState on Post and Group), so a naive component query is blind to entity type; the DSL builds from the entity's own default-scoped relation and adds only a correlated EXISTS/NOT EXISTS, so the entity-model scope (model = 'posts') applies automatically and a shared component cannot leak. Proven with Name (shared by User and Post in the gem fixtures) and in the demo with PublishState shared into Group. EXISTS chosen over JOIN (no duplicate rows, clean AND composition, null-safe NOT EXISTS). Conditions are sanitized through AR's where (verified a quote-bearing payload is escaped, not executed). The demo's Post.published was rewritten from the hand-rolled subquery (whose correctness silently rode the default_scope, a trap caught in review) to with_component(PublishState, state: 'published'). This closes the top backlog item; preloading (N+1) remains the next confirmed need.

Built component preloading (includes_components) — the last confirmed demo need, closing architecture.md open question 1. RFC-0011 + ADR-0012, 468 gem examples green. A spike up front reframed the whole feature: because components are has_one associations and the lazy reader (RFC-0006) calls super, ActiveRecord's NATIVE preload already works — User.all.preload(:name) drops 5 queries to 2 and a preloaded no-row component still reads as a virtual. So the feature is not machinery but a thin, discoverable, component-named wrapper: includes_components takes component classes (no args = all declared, walking inheritance), preloads via AR, validates each is declared on the entity with a component-shaped error rather than AR's AssociationNotFoundError, and chains. Half its value is regression tests pinning the native-preload/lazy-reader interaction so it can't silently break. The demo index now preloads its own components plus the nested author-name hop (preload(authorship: { author: :name }), standard AR nesting for a component reached through a relationship). The payoff is that it is bounded, not merely smaller: 2 posts 13->6 queries, 22 posts 93->6. The real HTTP index request went 14->8, constant in post count. The agent also caught two bugs in my RFC's own tests (an all-components example reading u.avatar.url on User, which doesn't declare Avatar; and an error-shape test stubbed with 'if false' that asserted nothing) — both fixed. v0.1 plus its two demo-driven enhancements (query DSL, preloading) are done; remaining backlog items (relationship DSL, required components, non-equality query conditions, Systems) are enhancements, not blockers.

Tagged the v0.1 milestone and wrote it up. ECS Rails v0.1 is feature-complete (11 RFCs, 12 ADRs, 468 examples against real PostgreSQL) and demo-validated by a working bulletin board built entirely on the gem; publishing to RubyGems remains a deliberate later step per ADR-0007, so the portfolio status stays 'building'. Deliverables: docs/retrospective-v0.1.md (the hypothesis 'is modelling a real Rails app out of components pleasant?', answered yes, with the process learnings and the roadmap), refreshed root and gem READMEs showing the real working API (lazy components, presence/markers, with_component/without_component, includes_components), and CHANGELOG 0.1.0. The retrospective's honest headline: across the build the design was wrong far more often than the code, and only execution caught it — two approved ADRs (0003, 0008) falsified by their own dependencies, an infinite recursion in the gem's own ADR-0006 example, and a test harness that silently swallowed rollbacks. The most compelling next item is the relationship DSL (the demo's Authorship/MemberUser/MemberGroup all reinvent belongs_to boilerplate, and it would enable a nested preloader for the last remaining N+1), deferred until the demo showed what it should look like — which it now has.

Built the relationship DSL (relates_to) — the one backlog item the demo actively argued for — and used it to clean up the demo. RFC-0012 + ADR-0013, superseding ADR-0006's deferral, 512 gem examples green. relates_to :author, User on an entity declares a cross-entity link with NO relationship component file: the DSL dynamically defines a backing component (Post::AuthorRelationship, table post_authors) with the belongs_to and declares it, so the whole stack (registry, lazy reader, delegation, with_component, presence, includes_components) applies for free. post.author / post.author= reach the target via delegation; a generator emits only the migration. The two FKs are deliberately asymmetric: cascade on the owner side (entity_id), nullify on the target side (author_id) — deleting the User nullifies the link but the Post survives, proven against the real DB. A spike up front confirmed feasibility before design. The agent found a real ADR bug: the backing reader is author_relationship only because relates_to pins the backing class's model_name to the demodulized element; the naive derivation yields post_author_relationship (namespace leaks in, owner name doubles), which would have silently broken the preload key. It also added InvalidRelationship < InvalidComponent (relationship-shaped message, still caught by rescue InvalidComponent). Demo cleanup: deleted authorship.rb/member_user.rb/member_group.rb, rewrote Post/Comment/Membership with relates_to (Membership is now three lines), regenerated the backing migrations, and confirmed the index still serves both published posts with author names in a bounded 8 queries. Next per the user: polish the demo UI to look great, be idiomatic Rails, and expose all features.

Polished the demo into a full, browsable bulletin board on a hand-written CSS design system (no framework), light/dark aware, exposing every gem feature. Built People (with moderator/admin badges = marker components, and promote/demote buttons wired to add/remove), Groups + Memberships (the join entity, add/remove members with a role), Comments and Likes on posts, and a 'how it works' explainer mapping each screen to the ECS model. Added Comment relates_to :post so comments belong to posts. Queries use the DSL throughout: a post's comments, a group's members, and a user's posts are all with_component on a relationship's backing column, and the index preloads to a bounded query count. Verified every flow end to end in the browser in both light and dark themes — post/comment/like creation, marker promote/demote (moderator? flips true/false), and an invalid email on the new-person form surfacing the merged component error (errors[:'email.address'] => 'is invalid'). One UI-phase finding logged: querying by a relationship leaks the backing class name (Comment::PostRelationship) and its raw column (post_id), which concretely argues for the relationship-name query sugar deferred from RFC-0012 — added to the backlog. Gem unchanged at 512 examples. This completes the user's two-part plan (relationship DSL, then UI polish); the demo now both models fully and looks the part.
